title: getStats() returns ratio=Infinity when originalBytes is forced to 0 by overflow guard
description: When width*height*bpp overflows Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER (e.g. 50000×50000 rgba16), the guard sets originalBytes=0 and then computes ratio = compressedBytes / 0 = Infinity. The EncodeStats contract documents ratio as 'compressedBytes / originalBytes', implying a finite number. Callers comparing ratio < 1.0 as a compression check will always be false, and logging/serialization (JSON.stringify) turns Infinity into null. The originalBytes=0 sentinel should also set compressedBytes=0 and ratio=0, or the function should return null.

title: DecodeSessionImpl exposes header() and info getter that are absent from the DecodeSession interface
description: DecodeSessionImpl.header() (line 192) and the info getter (line 188) are public methods callable on the concrete class but are not declared on the DecodeSession interface in jxl-core/src/types.ts. Code that holds a DecodeSession reference (returned by JxlContext.decode()) cannot call header() or read info without a cast. Any caller relying on these methods via the interface type will get a TypeScript compile error, and the methods may be silently dropped when the implementation is replaced. The DecodeSession interface should be extended, or these should be explicitly typed as implementation-only and not exported.

title: Large set of EncodeOptions fields are silently dropped and never forwarded to MsgEncodeStart
description: EncodeOptions in jxl-core/src/types.ts declares many fields (jpegReconstruction, alreadyDownsampled, upsamplingMode, ecResampling, frameIndexing, allowExpertOptions, buffering, modular, brotliEffort, decodingSpeed, photonNoiseIso, advancedControls) that are documented with WASM-bridge instructions. None of these fields appear in MsgEncodeStart (the protocol message) and none are forwarded in encode-session.ts. A caller who sets opts.jpegReconstruction or opts.buffering will receive no error and no effect — the feature silently does nothing. This is a contract promise gap: the public type advertises options that the session layer simply ignores.

title: pushPixels() after cancel/error throws the generic ConfigError, not the real terminal error
description: DecodeSessionImpl.push() was updated (DS-7) to surface the real terminal error from terminalError when the session is terminated. EncodeSessionImpl.pushPixels() (line 150-152) still throws a fixed ConfigError string regardless of why the session terminated — a cancelled session, an encode_error, or an OOM all produce the same 'ConfigError: pushPixels() after finish/cancel/error'. The two sibling implementations diverge on this contract point; encode callers cannot distinguish 'push after cancel' from 'push after OOM'.

title: decode_budget_exceeded frame is passed to makeFrame() but MsgDecodeBudgetExceeded does not extend DecodeFrameMeta
description: makeFrame() is typed to accept a base shape '& DecodeFrameMeta'. MsgDecodeBudgetExceeded carries only a region field (no sourceScale, progressiveSequence, passOrdinal, etc.) and does NOT extend DecodeFrameMeta in the protocol definition. TypeScript accepts the call because the DecodeFrameMeta fields are all optional, so the type is structurally satisfied. However the contract between protocol and session handler is implicit — if a future DecodeFrameMeta field becomes required, or if the worker starts emitting extra metadata on budget_exceeded, there is no compile-time guard that the message shape was updated. This is a latent drift risk between protocol.ts and the handler.

title: EncodeSessionImpl abort signal is checked after acquire rather than before, unlike DecodeSessionImpl
description: DecodeSessionImpl checks opts.signal.aborted BEFORE any scheduler interaction (lines 87-93 of decode-session.ts) and never acquires a slot for a pre-aborted signal. EncodeSessionImpl (lines 125-142) sets up the abort handler and then checks aborted, but by that point the abortHandler calls this.scheduler?.cancelSession() — which requires this.scheduler to already be set. For a pre-aborted signal, scheduler is still null at the time abortHandler() is called synchronously (line 136), so cancelSession is a no-op and the acquire promise is already in-flight. The slot may be acquired and never released if the abortHandler fires before initAcquire sets this.scheduler.

title: firstFrame() utility is exported from decode-session.ts but not re-exported from the package index
description: firstFrame() is a public utility function exported from packages/jxl-session/src/decode-session.ts (and appears in dist/decode-session.d.ts), but it is not re-exported from src/index.ts or src/browser.ts. External callers who import from '@casabio/jxl-session' cannot access firstFrame() without reaching into the internal module path. If any downstream code does import { firstFrame } from '@casabio/jxl-session/src/decode-session', it bypasses the public surface and breaks on any internal refactor.

category: enums handled by some consumers not others
title: encode_first_byte_ready message is received but its informational contract is silently dropped
description: The handler for encode_first_byte_ready (line 241) is a no-op comment saying 'time_to_first_byte_ms arrives via a metric message'. If the WASM bridge sends timing data on the encode_first_byte_ready message itself (e.g., a timeMs field), that data is discarded. More importantly, the EncodeSession interface and EncodeStats carry no 'timeToFirstByte' observable. Callers who want to know when first byte is ready must rely on the separate metric message, but the event type is defined in the protocol, implying a richer contract. This is a contract underspecification: the protocol defines the event but the session layer documents nothing about when/whether it fires or what callers can observe from it.

title: No contract test verifies that DecodeSessionImpl satisfies the DecodeSession interface at compile time
description: DecodeSession and EncodeSession interfaces live in jxl-core, but there is no assignability check (e.g. 'const _: DecodeSession = new DecodeSessionImpl(...)' or a typed cast) that would fail at compile time if the implementation drifts from the interface. The existing tests exercise behavior, but header() and info getter are accessible on the concrete class without any type error even though they are absent from the interface. A single const assignment in a test or types file would catch future divergence at compile time.

title: RequestedWorkerTier is a string union with no version guard; new tier values silently fall back to 'auto'
description: parseRequestedWorkerTier() accepts a jxlWorkerTier query parameter from a caller-supplied URL. Unknown tier strings silently return 'auto' (no error, no log). If a newer worker build adds a tier (e.g. 'avx512-mt') and the older session package is in use, the tier is silently ignored and the wrong worker variant is selected. The function has no mechanism to warn callers about unrecognized tiers. A console.warn in development mode for unrecognized-but-non-empty tier strings would catch mismatches at integration time.
